title: Where History and Ecology Meet: Daylighting Flushing Creek
description: In the early 1900s, Flushing Meadows–Corona Park was famously known as the “valley of ashes”—the wasteland immortalized in The Great Gatsby. Once wetlands along Flushing Creek, it was later buried under ash and rubble and transformed to host two World’s Fairs. Today, it’s one of NYC’s most diverse parks and one of the city’s most exposed assets to climate change and flood impacts. Join landscape architecture firm SCAPE on a walk through the park to learn how the park is being reimagined through daylighting as a more resilient destination—a process that will bring the river back to the surface and to a more natural state. Discover the park’s rich cultural history and how equitable, climate-adaptive design, ecology, public life, and infrastructure can coexist while restoring access to one of NY’s most cherished parks.
address: Tide Gate Bridge, Flushing, NY, USA

title: The Longest Short Walk in Brooklyn
description: Join this BLOCK WALK, a slow stroll across a single and singular block in Brooklyn: Eastern Parkway between Washington Avenue and Grand Army Plaza! In less than a third of a mile, attendees will move through (1) iconic cultural institutions such as the Brooklyn Museum, the Brooklyn Botanic Garden, Mount Prospect, and the Central branch of the Brooklyn Public Library, all while relishing (2) ordinary delights that only slow and close looking—especially looking together—can reveal.
address: Brooklyn Museum, Eastern Parkway, Brooklyn, NY, USA

title: Walking the Jewish Rialto: Yiddish Theater in NYC
description: Over a two hour walking tour, explore the heart of what was once a major hub for the sensational, international phenomenon known as Yiddish theater. Yiddish theater was the second most popular language theater in NYC with many theaters, troupes, and of course stars. Beginning at the site of Jacob Adler’s Grand Theater and ending in Union Square, this walking tour will have attendees walk along the former Yiddish Broadway with special attention paid to sites of past theaters and notable performances. Attendees should wear comfortable shoes, bring water, and be prepared to stand for longer periods of time.
address: 255 Grand Street, Manhattan, New York, NY 10002, USA

title: Olmsted's Ghost: Prospect Trace Western Half
description: Walk follows the western half of the Prospect Expressway as it transforms from a looming viaduct to an excavated trench. Walk will stop at local institutions that thrive in the shadow of the highway – Open Source Gallery, Butterfly Garden and Brooklyn Urban Garden Schools (BUGS).
address: Machate Circle, Park Cir, Brooklyn, NY 11218, USA

title: Radical Village
description: Join us for a walk around Greenwich Village as we explore the lives of the radical playwrights, lawyers, musicians, educators, artists, and activists who have called the neighborhood home. Among the sites we will visit: our city’s first integrated club where Billie Holiday debuted the anti-lynching anthem “Strange Fruit,” the Weather Underground townhouse that a bomb ripped apart, the prison where the FBI detained Angela Davis, the home of Pete Seeger and other artists targeted by the House Un-American Activities Committee, and more. Together we will reflect upon this community’s enduring legacy and impact on social movements of today.
address: 1 Sheridan Square, New York, NY, USA

title: Old Gods of Manhattan
description: New York claims to be a rationalist city of the future, so why are there old gods everywhere? Do these monuments simply offer familiarity, stability, and continuity to a city full of strangers? Or is there something deeper at play? Join us for a short walk in and around some of the city’s most iconic buildings, seeking to answer the question: why are these idols actually here? You’ll have a chance to share your thoughts. This is a conversation as much as a tour. We know you don’t want to go to 42nd Street on a Saturday, but this time it’ll be worth it. It might just change how you see these buildings, midtown, and New York.
address: 5 Av/W 41 St, New York, NY 10018, USA

title: Men with Painted Faces: Forgotten Stories From the “Fag Ward”
description: This guided walking tour uncovers a hidden chapter of LGBTQ history at the former Men’s Penitentiary on Welfare Island (now Roosevelt Island). Known as the “Fag Ward,” this segregated unit housed those imprisoned for homosexuality—individuals whose lives were criminalized, yet whose resilience gave rise to unexpected beauty and community. Though now demolished, attendees will walk the prison’s former footprint and hear how inmates labored in the laundry by day and, by night, the “Fag Ward” transformed into a vibrant underground culture—singing, dancing, and rehearsing for an annual Christmas vaudeville and drag pageant. The tour features artists behind THE GORGEOUS NOTHINGS, an upcoming Off-Broadway musical, alongside a companion podcast series by MAKING GAY HISTORY.
address: 455 Main St, New York, NY 10044, USA

title: Intentional Spacemaking: Public Space, Systems, and Us
description: Join us at the LeFrak Center in Prospect Park, Brooklyn to explore what it means to intentionally create public space for ourselves and our communities. Together we will look at why the space works or why it doesn’t through an Intentional Spacemaking™ lens (ie: systems integration of the physical container, activation, and context/care perspectives). How was this part of the park initially created? Who adapted it and why? Does the current design support local community? We’ll end with a chat about how we can take what we’ve learned about public spacemaking and bring it into our daily lives to support ourselves and our communities. Please come open and curious, ready to investigate the environment we are in, the many systems that affect and intersect that environment, and how we each experience and create space in the world.
address: 116 East Dr, Brooklyn, NY 11225, USA

title: Central Park’s North Woods: A Birding Paradise
description: The migration of birds from their southern wintering grounds to their northern breeding grounds is one of the most exciting spectacles of spring. Migration will be in full swing in early May as we explore the micro-hotspots of Central Park’s North Woods. We will be looking (and listening) for warblers, tanagers, orioles, and other colorful and vocal songbirds. This walk is open to anyone with an interest in learning more about the birds that pass through our city on their epic journey north. No prior experience or knowledge required! Attendees should wear comfortable shoes as we will be traversing some rocky terrain. Feel free to bring water/snacks and binoculars (if you have them). Unfortunately, binoculars will not be provided.
address: Central Park North/Malcolm X Blvd, New York, NY 10029, USA

title: Gotham Park, Beneath the Brooklyn Bridge
description: Join us to explore history in action – we are a grassroots effort unlocking the Manhattan Anchorage of the Brooklyn Bridge one acre at a time. Have you ever wondered how an infrastructural burden affects the neighborhood it occupies, and how surrounding communities can turn that burden into opportunity? Join us to learn about our adventures in history-mining, fun facts about the neighborhood and the Brooklyn Bridge, and how a band of determined community advocates are working with agency and City officials to unlock this iconic area beneath the shadow of one of our most beloved structures in the heart of Manhattan. And for skaters – yes, this includes the Brooklyn Banks!
address: City Hall Park Path & Centre Street, New York, NY, USA
locationName: City Hall Park Path & Centre Street

title: Discover an Overlooked Part of NYC's Historic Waterfront
description: Just behind Citi Field, the Mets' ballpark, you'll find the World's Fair Marina on Flushing Bay. While it's located across Roosevelt Ave. from the rest of Flushing Meadows Corona Park, the marina is part of the park and, like many other things in the park, is a remnant from the 1964 World's Fair. Today the marina is used by private boat owners, fishing and party boat excursions, and dragon boat races. while walkers, bicyclists, birders and picnickers enjoy the bayside promenade. Hear more about this area's history, find out about the ongoing shoreline restoration project, enjoy the view and get at the truth about the Candela structures – the most neglected and most misunderstood fair relics, which stand at the marina – on this waterfront walk.
address: Q533+52 New York, NY, USA

title: Pickle Paradise: A Walk Exploring New York City's “Pickle Alley”
description: New York is a city of immigrants, and those immigrants brought pickles with them. During the late 1800s and early 1900s, a heavy influx of Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe arrived to New York and, thankfully, introduced kosher dill pickles to America. On the Lower East Side especially, they quickly became a popular, affordable street food. Join journalist and avid pickle person Kasia Pilat on a walk exploring New York's “Pickle Alley,” a stretch of Essex Street where, at its peak, there were an estimated 80-plus pickle vendors. Learn about the history of the neighborhood, the pickling process, sip some pickle lemonade that Kasia will provide and visit some of the few remaining pickle shops in the area.
address: 317 Grand St, New York, NY 10002, USA

title: Pickle Paradise: A Walk Exploring New York City's “Pickle Alley”
description: New York is a city of immigrants, and those immigrants brought pickles with them. During the late 1800s and early 1900s, a heavy influx of Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe arrived to New York and, thankfully, introduced kosher dill pickles to America. On the Lower East Side especially, they quickly became a popular, affordable street food. Join journalist and avid pickle person Kasia Pilat on a walk exploring New York's “Pickle Alley,” a stretch of Essex Street where, at its peak, there were an estimated 80-plus pickle vendors. Learn about the history of the neighborhood, the pickling process, sip some pickle lemonade that Kasia will provide and visit some of the few remaining pickle shops in the area.
address: 309 Orchard St, New York, NY 10002, USA

title: Harlem in Vogue: Jazz, Ballrooms and the Streets That Shaped America
description: Step into Harlem, the epicenter of music, literature, and politics, where generations of artists, writers, and performers shaped American culture far beyond New York. Beginning at Strivers' Row, we pass the sites of the Cotton Club, Savoy Ballroom, and historic jazz clubs along Swing Street (W 133rd), move down Lenox Avenue, and end near the Apollo Theater. Along the way, we celebrate Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Thelonious Monk, Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Malcolm X, James Baldwin and others, while exploring Harlem’s queer expression, ballroom and jazz legacies. Clubs, churches, stoops, dance floors, apartments, and sidewalks reveal Harlem's enduring artistic, intellectual and literary power. Slow-paced, expect an immersive walk with stories, frequent stops and conversation.
address: 2610 Frederick Douglass Blvd, New York, NY 10030, USA

title: Harlem in Vogue: Jazz, Ballrooms and the Streets That Shaped America
description: Step into Harlem, the epicenter of music, literature, and politics, where generations of artists, writers, and performers shaped American culture far beyond New York. Beginning at Strivers' Row, we pass the sites of the Cotton Club, Savoy Ballroom, and historic jazz clubs along Swing Street (W 133rd), move down Lenox Avenue, and end near the Apollo Theater. Along the way, we celebrate Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Thelonious Monk, Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Malcolm X, James Baldwin and others, while exploring Harlem’s queer expression, ballroom and jazz legacies. Clubs, churches, stoops, dance floors, apartments, and sidewalks reveal Harlem's enduring artistic, intellectual and literary power. Slow-paced, expect an immersive walk with stories, frequent stops and conversation.
address: 2610 Frederick Douglass Blvd, New York, NY 10030, USA

title: Prospect Park Monuments of Black Americans
description: New York City is blessed with an incredible array of public sculptures. One overlooked aspect of this collection is its monuments of Black Americans, each with its own remarkable story. Local author David Felsen leads a tour based on his recently published book, “New York City Monuments of Black Americans: A History and Guide” in which attendees will walk from the Soldiers and Sailors Memorial Arch in Grand Army Plaza through the park to the Marquis de Lafayette Memorial on 9th St and Prospect Park West. Attendees will learn the history and significance of both of these memorials and gain a whole new perspective on the Black characters in them.
address: 3 Eastern Pkwy, Brooklyn, NY 11238, USA

title: Tour of the Shirley Chisholm Recreation Center
description: Join us for a tour of the new Shirley Chisholm Recreation Center, designed by Studio Gang. Named in honor of the Brooklyn-born activist, educator, and first Black woman to serve in US Congress, the Shirley Chisholm Recreation Center creates a welcoming social hub for fitness, recreation, and learning in the East Flatbush neighborhood of Brooklyn. The 74,000 sf building’s sculpted architecture and transparent features invite residents into a new public space that supports health and community life. Lower levels offer a gymnasium, walking track, and a double-height competition-grade swimming pool, among other spaces dedicated to recreation and exercise. The upper level includes naturally daylit learning spaces, which continue outside onto a wrap-around rooftop terrace.
address: 3105 Farragut Place, Brooklyn, NY 11210, USA

title: One Block, 400 Years of History
description: This one-hour tour will circle the block bounded by West 106th and 107th Streets and by West End Ave. and Riverside Dr. We’ll see how the jumble of building types and architectural styles provides a rich narrative of growth and decline, waves of immigration, and evolving social values. We’ll discuss the Dutch and English settlers who farmed the land along with enslaved people from Africa, the site’s role in a 1776 battle, and how Riverside Park and the IRT transformed the site from a country estate to a community of townhouses and apartment buildings. Architectural highlights include the mansions built for a tobacco tycoon and a Shakespearean actress.
address: Straus Park, West 107th Street, New York, NY, USA

title: One Block, 400 Years of History
description: This one-hour tour will circle the block bounded by West 106th and 107th Streets and by West End Ave. and Riverside Dr. We’ll see how the jumble of building types and architectural styles provides a rich narrative of growth and decline, waves of immigration, and evolving social values. We’ll discuss the Dutch and English settlers who farmed the land along with enslaved people from Africa, the site’s role in a 1776 battle, and how Riverside Park and the IRT transformed the site from a country estate to a community of townhouses and apartment buildings. Architectural highlights include the mansions built for a tobacco tycoon and a Shakespearean actress.
address: Straus Park, West 107th Street, New York, NY, USA

title: The Gowanus Lowlands: From Industrial Canal to New Civic Landscape
description: Building on decades of city, state, and federal planning efforts, the Gowanus Lowlands is a community-based vision for a restored public realm centered around Brooklyn’s Gowanus Canal—a federal Superfund site and former marshland in a neighborhood recently rezoned for over 8,500 new housing units. Anticipating a neighborhood in transition, the Gowanus Lowlands is an action plan for positive change. The plan guides the design of an ecologically resilient and socially robust park network along the canal, coordinating the actions of separate landowners into a cohesive waterfront park. Join team members from landscape architecture firm SCAPE, the Gowanus Canal Conservancy, and NYC Department of City Planning on a tour of recently completed public landscapes, and learn about the ecological future of the canal.
address: 420 Carroll St, Brooklyn, NY 11215, USA

title: Dead Dutch and Still Around
description: The Midwood and Flatlands sections of Brooklyn were both established as farming communities in the 1600s by families from the Netherlands whose names now serve as placemarks in these neighborhoods. The walking tour begins at Amersfort Park, named after Amersfoort, a city in the Netherlands. The Park consists of land deeded to the city by the Hubbard family with unbreakable stipulations. This is one example that In this neighborhood, the Dutch, though long since dead, are very much, still around. We will walk to Flatlands Dutch Reformed Church on Kings Highway and 40th Street. We will spend some time in the graveyard, where members of noted Dutch families rest. If time permits will enter the church towards the end of the Sunday service and a member of the church will provide a brief history of the Lutheran Church in Brooklyn for our tour.
address: Amersfort Park, East 38th Street, Brooklyn, NY, USA

title: Fly Off Course: Disc Golf & the History of Highland Park
description: Come fly off course. Join Alex Bender, co-founder of the NYC Disc Golf Association and designer of NYC's only disc golf course, for a walk through Highland Park that blends sport, history, and community. We'll explore the park's rich history, from its Depression-era stone bridge to its ecology, to its role in Brooklyn-Queens daily life, while learning how to play disc golf and how it finally came to New York City. You'll play the actual course; discs provided, no experience needed. Wear comfortable shoes and be ready to throw. Meet at the Google Maps pin for “Highland Park Disc Golf Course”, near the baseball field at Highland Blvd. and Heath Pl. Open to all ages and abilities. Sloped, trail-like terrain. Transit accessible.
address: M4P5+35 New York, NY, USA

title: Fly Off Course: Disc Golf & the History of Highland Park
description: Come fly off course. We'll explore the park's rich history, from its Depression-era stone bridge to its ecology, to its role in Brooklyn-Queens daily life, while learning how to play disc golf and how it finally came to New York City. Transit accessible.
address: 267 Highland Blvd, Brooklyn, NY 11207, USA

title: A Wild Woman's Walk On The Upper West Side
description: Wear walking shoes and bring water for this leisurely 1 1/2 hour tour. We will stop at: Museum Of Natural History Beresford Apartments Belvedere Castle Shakespeare Garden Bethesda Fountain Strawberry Fields The Dakota Strawberry Fields The Dakota
address: 81 St - Museum of Natural History, New York, NY 10024, USA

title: Heights Heritage Tour
description: In-person walking tour of the Washington Heights Community. Starting at the Fort Washington Branch and walking from 181st Street to 155th Street. We will concentrate on pointing out the landmarks and giving a brief description of each along the way.
address: Fort Washington Library, West 179th Street, New York, NY, USA

title: Explore Historic Fordham Road with a 2 Mile Walking Tour
description: We will take a walk through the Fordham Road area starting at The Bronx Library Center (BLC), Poe Park, St. James Park, Post Office, Monroe University, Fordham Shopping, Bryan Park, 1 Fordham Plaza, Fordham U Walsh Library, Metro North, and ending at BLC.
address: Bronx Library Center, East Kingsbridge Road, The Bronx, NY, USA

title: Waterfront Walking Tour: Art and History in Battery Park City
description: Join a librarian from the Battery Park City Library for an engaging, one-hour walking tour to explore the unique history, key landmarks, and vibrant public art of the waterfront Battery Park City neighborhood. The in-person walk begins at the library, tracing the neighborhood’s development with stops at Teardrop Park, Rockefeller Park, and the Irish Hunger Memorial. Get ready to spot the mischievous miniature bronze figures from Tom Otterness' “The Real World” exhibit!
address: New York Public Library - Battery Park City Library, 175 North End Avenue, New York, NY 10282, USA

title: Tottenville Trot 2026: A Charming Walk Through Historic Tottenville
description: Take a leisurely little trot through the streets of downtown Tottenville in the company of friendly local library staff! Join branch staff from historic Tottenville Public Library for an in person walking tour of Tottenville: The Town the Oyster Built! Learn more about the history, landmarks, environment, and community of this charming seaside neighborhood. We'll take a walk from the historic landmark Tottenville Public Library, around the heart of town, down Main Street, to the Compass House and the original Totten Dock area.​ The tour will last approximately 1 hour and will begin at our neighborhood library.
address: Tottenville Library, Amboy Road, Staten Island, NY, USA

title: Walk the Hall of Fame at Bronx Community College
description: Join us for a historical walk of Bronx Community Campus. Learn some of the history behind its beautiful architecture, enjoy the view and take a picture of the Hall of Fame for Great Americans!
address: 2150 University Avenue, The Bronx, NY, USA

title: Not an Insurrection, an Uprising: NYC's First Rebellion of Enslaved Africans
description: According to Brittanica.com, the events on April 6, 1712, were a violent insurrection. Revolt, rebellion, uprising. Definitions matter, especially if you must take your fight for liberty and the pursuit of happiness into your own hands. Trump, termed the insurrection at our nation's capital on January 6, 2021, “A Day of Love.” “No lie can live forever.” M.L. King, Jr. On the night of April 6, 1712, a violent rebellion (or a righteous act of self-affirming love and humanity) by enslaved Africans took place in lower Manhattan. Join us for a talk and a walk in commemoration of the bravery of these New Yorkers. The events of the rebellion will be presented in detail and may trigger strong reactions. We will walk to a site to pour libations and pray for the souls of the rebels and those currently fighting for their freedom/liberty
address: 125 Maiden Lane, New York, NY 10038, USA

title: Harlem's Public Art & Architecture
description: The first stop will be at the NYPL George Bruce Library on 125th St and Amsterdam. We will then visit two public art sculptures, walk by the Apollo Theater and Studio Museum, then go back west to see the From Harlem With Love mural at 125th St and Old Broadway.
address: New York Public Library, 518 W 125th St, New York, NY 10027, USA

title: Edible Education: Building and Maintaining a School Garden in NYC
description: This tour explores Edible Schoolyard NYC’s largest garden at PS 216 in Brooklyn—a former lot now a vibrant space with orchard & herb garden. This tour will explore Edible Schoolyard NYC’s oldest and largest school garden at PS 216 in Gravesend, Brooklyn. The half-acre garden, which was converted from a parking lot in 2010, is now home to a robust pollinator garden, greenhouse, vegetable and herb beds, cut flowers, and a thriving orchard, including fruit trees such as figs, pawpaws, pomegranates, persimmons, and pears. The garden hosts Pre-K through 5th-grade classes, a student garden club, weekly volunteers, community open garden hours, and a farmstand during the growing season.
address: P.S. 216 Arturo Toscanini, Avenue X, Brooklyn, NY, USA

title: Grounded in Resiliency: A Community Composting Tour
description: Visitors will have the opportunity to take a tour of Frank White Memorial, where they will experience how we integrate youth programming into our garden, giving young people the power to take agency over the projects they create. Feeding into our community composting efforts, participants will also be able to see how we couple composting in our growing spaces with processing organic material from start to finish.
address: Frank White Memorial Garden, 506 W 143rd St, New York, NY 10031, USA

title: Soil Start Farm and Compost Learning Center
description: We will have our Green Thumb apprentices co-lead a farm and compost tour with a hands on activity component. GreenThumb apprentices will co-lead a farm and compost tour featuring a hands-on learning component. At our Earth Matter site, participants will explore different backyard composting systems, learn how to create a nutrient-rich, compost-infused seed-starting mix, and gain practical skills for growing from seed. The session will conclude with a take-home activity, where each participant prepares and plants their own seeded pot using the mix they’ve created—offering a simple, accessible way to continue the learning at home.
address: Governors Island, New York, NY, USA

title: Discovering BBG's Discovery Garden
description: Encourage children with hands-on experiences exploring plants and animals in their habitat. This is an adult only session. Brooklyn Botanic Garden Discovery Garden offers countless lessons in engaging children with the world of plants. This immersive landscape—designed especially for the Garden’s youngest visitors—invites exploration through hands-on experiences, encouraging children to investigate plants and animals across diverse habitat spaces. Join this tour to gain inspiration and practical ideas for engaging children in your own garden or educational setting. Please note: this tour is intended for adults.
address: Brooklyn Botanic Garden, Brooklyn, NY, USA

title: Gardens of the Bronx River Greenway by Bicycle
description: We will bike from Shoelace Park to Soundview Park and explore how NYC Parks and The Bronx River Alliance steward their gardens. Join the Urban Park Rangers for a guided bike tour along the gardens of the Bronx River Greenway. Traveling from Shoelace Park to Soundview Park, participants will explore how NYC Parks, the Bronx River Alliance, and local volunteers steward green spaces and help make the greenway more vibrant and resilient.This tour highlights the “how” and “why” behind caring for urban park landscapes, with stops showcasing a range of ecological features and community-led efforts. Proposed stops include green infrastructure in Shoelace Park, the dog run or forested areas of the Bronx River Forest, gardens at Ranaqua (Bronx Parks Headquarters), Bronx River Community Garden, Drew Gardens, Bronx River House, The Foodway at Conc
address: Shoelace Park, Bronx River Parkway, The Bronx, NY, USA

title: Remembering Staten Island: Green Space Organizing For Change
description: This tour is led by members of the Hill Street Garden Collective, a resident-led coalition of garden members, youth, and caregivers working to repair Hill Street Garden’s image, leadership, and systems. Participants will visit Hill Street Garden to learn how residents are organizing to expand access, shared governance, and food sovereignty in a borough often framed as distant or disconnected from movement work. The tour recontextualizes the “Forgotten Borough” by highlighting longstanding Black, Brown, and working-class immigrant organizing traditions and positioning green space as a site of repair, leadership development, and collective care.
address: 705 Forest Avenue, Staten Island, NY, USA

title: The Path to Mycelium
description: Join the New York Restoration Project's urban agriculture team for a comprehensive tour of our beloved Teaching Garden. Learn about how we cultivate shiitake mushrooms at the largest outdoor log yard in New York City. And explore other interesting things growing in the garden, from ginger and turmeric to chayote and more.
address: Riley-Levin Children's Garden, 10th Avenue, New York, NY, USA

title: Habitat Restoration in an Urban Setting
description: This session explores the fundamentals of creating native plant habitats within the unique conditions of an urban public park, such as Fraser Square Park. Participants will learn how to design and establish plantings that support native wildlife, with a focus on birds and beneficial invertebrates such as pollinators.
address: Fraser Square, Avenue M, Brooklyn, NY, USA

title: Native Trees, Plants & the Ways They Were Consumed by NY Indigenous Nations
description: An immersive tour by ethnobotanists. Uncover how regional Indigenous people cultivated plants for food, medicine, tools, and ceremony. Step into Prospect Park with an experienced ethnobotanist for an immersive walking tour exploring the park’s rich native plant life. With Rooting Together, participants will learn to identify a diverse range of species growing all around us while gaining insight into how regional Indigenous cultures have cultivated and used these plants for food, medicine, tools, and ceremony. Blending ecology, subsistence practices, history, and cultural knowledge, this guided experience offers a deeper understanding of the land beneath our feet and the enduring relationships between people and plants. Ideal for nature lovers, educators, and anyone curious about Indigenous knowledge systems and urban ecology.
address: 20 Grand Army Plz, Brooklyn, NY 11238, USA

title: Explore NYC's Urban Farm
description: The Randall's Island Parks Alliance invites visitors to deepen their connection with NYC-grown food on our one-acre community-focused farm and outdoor classroom. Since its inception in 2006, the Urban Farm has grown in size and scope, now featuring over 100 raised beds, two greenhouses, four rice paddies, and a small fruit tree orchard. We grow over 200 varieties of fruits and vegetables from around the world, with a focus on crops that are culturally relevant to our nearby communities in East Harlem, the South Bronx, and Western Queens. How do we incorporate all of this into our educational programming for all ages? Come stroll the Urban Farm with us and find out!
address: Randall's Island Park Alliance, Central Road, New York, NY, USA

title: Pollinator Pockets in Tompkins Square Park: A Walk & Talk
description: “Walk our Pollinator Pocket gardens in Tompkins Square Park to see resilient, local plants supporting pollinators and ecological restoration. Join us for a walk-and-talk through the newly established Pollinator Pocket gardens in Tompkins Square Park, a 10.5-acre green space in the East Village. These gardens are central to the ecological restoration of the park’s southeast quadrant following severe damage from Hurricane Isaias. Planted with tough, locally sourced species, the Pollinator Pockets are designed to support birds, bees, butterflies, and other beneficial insects while helping to reduce flooding, withstand intense summer heat, and provide four-season interest. During the tour, we will share lessons learned about selecting and caring for plants that thrive in a busy urban park amid a changing climate. We’ll also highlight the p
address: 116 Avenue A, New York, NY 10009, USA

title: Rooted In Diversity Garden Tour
description: Tour our garden to see how food, culture, and community grow together and learn why our plants matter to different cultures. Hey Everyone! On the Rooted in Diversity Garden Tour, we’ll take a stroll through our shared garden and see how food, culture, and community are all growing side by side. You’ll get to know the plants we grow, why they matter to different cultures, and how we use them. We’ll share stories, swap easy tips for your own home garden, and talk about how this green space brings us all together. And of course, we’ll wrap it up with some laughs, questions, and maybe a little inspiration to grow something beautiful at home!
address: Diversity Edible Farm Garden, 1673 Madison Ave, New York, NY 10029, USA

title: A Garden At The End of the World
description: Meet at an intersection of historical + cultural land. Put it in the context of modern-day resilience, biodiversity, & rewilding context. Stewarding the land begins with respecting a place's ecological and cultural past and imagining a future of enduring ecosystem value. Walking from a former Art in the Parks project site in McGolrick Park to the industrial stretch of Greenpoint set against the NYC skyline, we will explore the transformative power of adopting overlooked spaces in the public realm. Visiting the site of a native plant garden in the making, we will find ourselves at a literal intersection of historical land use regulations and cultural land consumption, and ground it in the context of modern-day resilience frameworks, biodiversity, and rewilding.
address: McGolrick Park, Nassau Avenue, Brooklyn, NY, USA

title: East Village Community Gardens
description: The East Village is home to a vibrant collection of community gardens, each a unique reflection of its gardeners' creativity and passion. With a longstanding artistic tradition, the neighborhood’s gardens serve as an extension of that rich cultural heritage. This tour offers both out-of-towners and locals from other boroughs a chance to experience the beauty and ingenuity behind these green spaces, showcasing some of the most stunning and distinctive gardens in the city. Join us as we explore iconic community gardens that embody the spirit and pride of the East Village.
address: 6th Street & Avenue B Community Garden, Avenue B, New York, NY, USA

title: Claiming Our Future – Elmhurst's Traffic Advocacy
description: A two mile route around Jackson Heights and Elmhurst with a focus on traffic safety advocacy, which has led to award winning designs, open streets, unique road use and the full use of the DOT tool kit for street design. Art along the route consists of the “Thank You” sign displayed publicly for Elmhurst Hospital during the Covid crisis, a ghost bike, HORT society plantings, possible folk dancing/exercise of Asian community in Elmhurst and scale model of Elmhurst Hospital. This walk is co-hosted by Cristina Furlong of New York Coalition for Transportation Safety and Make Queens Safer, along with Elmhurst Hospital Committee on Trauma who will walk with us, and chat about how to reduce trauma injuries as a cyclist, micromobility user, driver or pedestrian. Please dress for weather, and bring water.
address: 79-01 Broadway, Elmhurst. Community Entrance to Elmhurst Hospital Center

title: Fantasy Townhouses Along Riverside Drive
description: We will walk along Riverside Drive and West End Avenue from 72nd to 79th Streets exploring mansions that exploded in the neighborhood from the 1880s through the early 20th Century designed by architects famous and forgotten. The arrival of the subway along Broadway in 1904 increased the value of real estate making individual townhouses uneconomical and kicking off a spasm of apartment buildings. We will talk about a few of those apartment buildings, about Riverside Park and its creators and about the history of the neighborhood. There will also be some gossip about a few neighborhood residents.
address: 72nd Street and Riverside Drive, Northeast corner new york city

title: Historic Flatiron District Walking Tour
description: Join our professional guides on a 90-minute journey through this vibrant neighborhood, viewing some of the City’s most notable landmarks, including the New York Life Insurance Building, the MetLife Clock Tower, the Appellate Courthouse, and the famous Flatiron Building. The walking tour takes place rain or shine. Comfortable walking shoes are recommended.
address: Flatiron Building, 5th Avenue, New York, NY, USA

title: Finding Colonial Jamaica, Queens
description: In the current era of urbanization of downtown Jamaica, this walk will emphasize existing and former structures (such as churches, cemeteries, houses, ponds, and civic organizations) that emerged during the colonial era. To commemorate the 250th Anniversary of the American Revolution, tourists will be introduced to events in Jamaica, Queens that served as turning points in the American Revolution. Famous people (such as George Washington and Rufus King) and their connections to Jamaica will be highlighted.
address: 155-15 Jamaica Avenue, Jamaica, NY, USA

title: Pest Propaganda: An Ode to the Rat & Pigeon
description: Join us to celebrate our city’s most-reviled residents: vermin. On this walk, we’ll discuss why rats and pigeons are the truest New Yorkers. Discover how our scrappy, cosmopolitan, and opportunistic non-human neighbors have made the city their home, and the tricks they use to not only survive, but thrive, in an environment built for humans. Our carefully-curated pest propaganda will leave you with a newfound appreciation for the nuisance next door! We’ll be walking through Sara Delano Roosevelt Park & Liz Christy Community Garden, stopping to spot signs of our non-human neighbors while completing an interactive bingo. There will be prizes! (No animal interactions required.)
address: 115 E Houston St, New York, NY 10002, USA

title: 125th Street Mural Tour
description: Our Jane’s Walk on Harlem’s famous 125th St will visit 7+ large-scale murals created over the last 20 years. We will explore these very different works of art developed by Creative Art Works and its youth apprentices. Topics will include the mural-making process, subject content and technique, and personal narratives of the artists and other stakeholders. CAW has developed hundreds of celebrated murals around the City in iconic places, e.g. Rockefeller Center and Citicorp Center; we will stop along our Walk route to discuss these stunning 7 very relevant murals, among others, at retailers Whole Foods, Trader Joes and Shack Shack and on walls controlled by Harlem Community Development Corp and DOT. We will delve into placemaking and how public art can activate, influence culture and brand spaces.
address: 52c Dr Martin Luther King Jr Blvd, New York, NY 10035, USA

title: From Marsh to Meadow: A Spring Nature Walk Through Randall’s Island
description: Nestled in the heart of New York City, Randall’s Island comes alive each spring as tidal marshes, shorelines, and meadows burst into color and activity. Join us for a guided nature walk of NYC’s largest salt marsh and our award-winning waterfront garden, where budding trees, blooming flowers, and returning wildlife mark the changing season. Begin at Little Hell Gate Salt Marsh to see how this vital ecosystem supports biodiversity, mitigates climate change, and serves as a nursery for local species. Learn about the island’s rich natural history and encounter some of the creatures that call it home. Then continue into the Waterfront Garden, where spring is in bloom. address: Little Hell Gate Salt Marsh, Little Hell Gate Bridge, New York, NY, USA

title: Women Who Wowed
description: Reserve your space today for the Museum’s abbreviated 45-minute version of the Women Who Wowed walking tour. Together we will wind through the cobblestones of the South Street Seaport Historic District uncovering the often under-recognized contributions of the women who helped build this city. Advanced registration is required through the MAS website for this free tour. Rain or shine.
address: 200 Front St, New York, NY 10038, USA

title: Harlem's Hamilton Heights and Sugar Hill
description: Join us as we discover some of Manhattan's hidden treasures along with the history and current lifestyle! Encompassing both sides of Broadway this unique and diverse Manhattan neighborhood contains some of the city's most beautiful townhouses, unique architecture, historic sites and Manhattan's only state park, which is a vital community hub. We'll stop by The Grange, the only home owned by Alexander Hamilton, community gardens and an art gallery that specializes in artists of African descent. Along our route there are distinctive houses of worship and the stunning campus of the City College of New York. Note indoor access to The Grange and other locations will be subject to the respective group visit policy. Bring snacks and water to drink, sun protection recommended.
address: Meet at 145th Street & Riverside Drive, entrance to Denny Farrell Riverbank State Park
